content.text: Special tokens are reserved vocabulary entries that carry control signals to the model rather than linguistic content. Unlike normal tokens, they are never produced by applying BPE or WordPiece to text — they are injected by the tokeniser at specific structural positions. The model is trained to understand their meaning: a
tells the model a turn has ended, and a
switches the model into fill-in-the-middle (FIM) mode. Getting special tokens right is critical when fine-tuning, when running inference on open-weight models, or when building a custom tokeniser.
Mark start and end of a document or full conversation; help the model learn document boundaries during pretraining
Separate system/user/assistant turns; the model learns to generate text only after the assistant delimiter
Signal that the current speaker has finished; model uses this as a stopping criterion during generation
Fill shorter sequences in a batch to uniform length; masked in attention (attention_mask = 0)
Used in code models (StarCoder, GPT-4) to request a completion that fits between a prefix and a suffix
Delimit function-call JSON blobs in models fine-tuned with tool use (model-specific)

from transformers import AutoTokenizer

models = [
    "bert-base-uncased",
    "t5-small",
    "gpt2",
    "meta-llama/Meta-Llama-3-8B-Instruct",
]

for model_name in models:
    tok = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained(model_name)
    print(f"\n{model_name}")
    print(f"  bos_token:  {tok.bos_token!r:20s}  id={tok.bos_token_id}")
    print(f"  eos_token:  {tok.eos_token!r:20s}  id={tok.eos_token_id}")
    print(f"  pad_token:  {tok.pad_token!r:20s}  id={tok.pad_token_id}")
    print(f"  unk_token:  {tok.unk_token!r:20s}  id={tok.unk_token_id}")

# bert-base-uncased
#   bos_token:  '[CLS]'               id=101
#   eos_token:  '[SEP]'               id=102
#   pad_token:  '[PAD]'               id=0
#   unk_token:  '[UNK]'               id=100
#
# t5-small
#   bos_token:  None                  id=None
#   eos_token:  '</s>'                id=1
#   pad_token:  '<pad>'               id=0
#   unk_token:  '<unk>'               id=2
#
# gpt2
#   bos_token:  '<|endoftext|>'       id=50256
#   eos_token:  '<|endoftext|>'       id=50256  ← same token for both
#   pad_token:  None                  id=None   ← GPT-2 has no pad token!
#   unk_token:  '<|endoftext|>'       id=50256
#
# meta-llama/Meta-Llama-3-8B-Instruct
#   bos_token:  '<|begin_of_text|>'   id=128000
#   eos_token:  '<|end_of_text|>'     id=128001
#   pad_token:  '<|end_of_text|>'     id=128001
#   unk_token:  None                  id=None

from transformers import AutoTokenizer
import torch

tok = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained("gpt2")

# GPT-2 has no pad token — a common pain point when batching.
# The standard fix: reuse the EOS token as PAD.
tok.pad_token = tok.eos_token
print(f"pad_token: {tok.pad_token!r}  id={tok.pad_token_id}")

# Adding a brand-new special token (e.g. for a custom fine-tune)
new_tokens = ["<|thinking|>", "<|/thinking|>", "<|answer|>"]
num_added = tok.add_special_tokens({"additional_special_tokens": new_tokens})
print(f"Added {num_added} new special tokens. New vocab size: {len(tok)}")
# New vocab size: 50260

# IMPORTANT: after adding tokens you must resize the model's embedding matrix
# model.resize_token_embeddings(len(tok))
# Otherwise the new token IDs are out of range for the embedding table.

# Check that the special token is NOT tokenised as sub-pieces:
text = "Hello <|thinking|> world"
with_special = tok.encode(text, add_special_tokens=False)
without_special_flag = tok.encode("<|thinking|>", add_special_tokens=False)
print(tok.convert_ids_to_tokens(with_special))
# ['Hello', 'Ġ<|thinking|>', 'Ġworld']  — treated as a single token

# Contrast with encoding the characters as regular text (before adding):
# This would split '<|thinking|>' into ['<', '|', 'th', 'inking', '|', '>']

FIM is a pretraining objective where the model learns to predict a middle span given its prefix and suffix context. Code completion models like StarCoder and the Codex family are trained with FIM using a specific special-token framing. The PSM (prefix-suffix-middle) format is the most common:

# StarCoder / SantaCoder FIM format (PSM: prefix–suffix–middle)
# pip install transformers
from transformers import AutoTokenizer, AutoModelForCausalLM
import torch

tok = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained("bigcode/starcoderbase-1b")
# model = AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained("bigcode/starcoderbase-1b")

FIM_PREFIX = "<fim_prefix>"
FIM_SUFFIX = "<fim_suffix>"
FIM_MIDDLE = "<fim_middle>"

def build_fim_prompt(prefix: str, suffix: str) -> str:
    """
    Build a fill-in-the-middle prompt.
    The model is expected to generate the middle content.
    """
    return f"{FIM_PREFIX}{prefix}{FIM_SUFFIX}{suffix}{FIM_MIDDLE}"

prefix = "def fibonacci(n: int) -> int:\n    "
suffix = "\n    return result"

prompt = build_fim_prompt(prefix, suffix)
print("FIM prompt:")
print(prompt)
print()
print("Tokens:")
print(tok.tokenize(prompt))
# ['<fim_prefix>', 'def', 'Ġfibonacci', '(', 'n', ':', 'Ġint', ')', ...,
#  '<fim_suffix>', 'Ġreturn', 'Ġresult', '<fim_middle>']
# The model generates the function body between prefix and suffix.
During autoregressive generation, the model emits tokens one by one. The inference loop must stop when the model produces the EOS token (or a role-end token like
). Failing to configure stop tokens correctly produces runaway generation or truncated output:

from transformers import AutoTokenizer, AutoModelForCausalLM, TextStreamer
import torch

model_id = "meta-llama/Meta-Llama-3-8B-Instruct"
tok   = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained(model_id)
# model = AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained(model_id, torch_dtype=torch.bfloat16)

# LLaMA 3 uses two stop tokens:
# 128001: <|end_of_text|>  — hard document end
# 128009: <|eot_id|>       — end of a single turn (more common during chat)
stop_ids = [
    tok.eos_token_id,                       # <|end_of_text|>: 128001
    tok.convert_tokens_to_ids("<|eot_id|>"),  # 128009
]
print("Stop token IDs:", stop_ids)  # [128001, 128009]

# In model.generate():
# outputs = model.generate(
#     input_ids,
#     max_new_tokens=512,
#     eos_token_id=stop_ids,   # list accepted since transformers 4.39
#     do_sample=True,
#     temperature=0.6,
# )

# When using an OpenAI-compatible API you can also pass stop strings:
# openai_client.chat.completions.create(
#     ...,
#     stop=["<|eot_id|>", "<|end_of_text|>"],
# )

Special tokens are the interface between the chat format the developer writes and the flat token stream the transformer processes. When they are correct, the model knows when each speaker starts and stops, where to generate, and when to halt. When they are wrong — a missing BOS token, a pad token that matches the EOS, or a FIM prefix injected into the wrong position — the model receives malformed input and its behaviour becomes unpredictable. Every open-weight model has its own special token conventions; always read the model card before writing a custom inference loop.
